evaluation_inputs.system_description: An Event framework designed to handle and process events for an event-driven application. The framework consists of an Event Manager, Event Queue, and Handlers. The application utilizes these services to process user inputs from a command-line interface, binding events to specific handlers via a registry.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Fully developed architecture with class diagrams, component descriptions, and defined component interactions.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: Can the components of the architecture be reused for future projects?
Can the framework be expanded in the future to accommodate a new application or new components?
Will the system handle any input given by the user and handle invalid input?
Is the architecture consistent in behavior?
Can any new application-specific functionality be added to the architecture?
Can the system be modified in short time and cost-effective manner?
Does the architecture perform its event-processing task properly?

hardware_view_description: System accepts input via Command Line Interface (CLI)
Single processor execution implied (sequential event processing)
runtime_constraints: Events must be dispatched in a First-Come-First-Served (FIFO) pattern
System must maintain an 'idle wait' state until input is received
Invalid inputs must be handled gracefully

quantitative_model_parameters.performance_proxy: Number of components involved in a transaction
quantitative_model_parameters.transaction_speed: Qualitative (Fastest possible based on minimal component interaction)

questions: Does the system allow unauthorized access?
Does the architecture portray data confidentiality?
Does the architecture process any task in the fastest possible speed?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: No unauthorized access to the system
All operations are processed in fastest possible speed
Any failure should be followed by immediate recovery
User errors in using system should be handled
New demands on system functionality should be accommodated
Major parts of the framework should be re-usable for future architectures
Framework modification is cost-effective, fast and in minimal time
Components in framework interact in a coordinated manner
Framework expansion is possible to allow for more sophisticated options
Implementation allows for execution in different environments
Proper data encapsulation and secure data structures
Flexible implementation using other programming languages
Overall consistent behavior expected from the architecture
Framework should be complete, clear and perform exactly as required
Hook up multiple applications to the framework at same time
Change interface from command-driven to window-based

business_goals: Provide an Event framework to be used by an application
Handle and process events through component interaction
Allow application components to utilize the framework services
technical_constraints: Must handle system termination via STOP handler
Must maintain system in idle state until input is received

architectural_approaches.description: The system uses an Event Manager to coordinate event processing, an Event Queue for FIFO event handling, and a Handler Registry pattern to bind events to specific handlers. Components interact through event dispatching mechanism.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Event-handler binding through registry allows new handlers to be added without modifying core framework
Framework can be reused across different applications by registering different event handlers
FIFO queue ensures predictable event processing order and graceful handling of invalid inputs
Consistent event-driven paradigm throughout the system maintains architectural coherence and simplifies understanding

children.scenario_description: Major parts of the framework should be re-usable for future architectures
Framework modification is cost-effective, fast and in minimal time
New event handlers can be added without modifying core framework
New demands on system functionality should be accommodated
FIFO queue ensures ordered event handling
User errors in using system should be handled without system failure
Components in framework interact in a coordinated manner through uniform event-driven pattern
Consistent architectural approach reduces cognitive complexity for developers
All operations are processed in fastest possible speed through minimal component interaction

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer needs to reuse the Event Manager, Event Queue, and Handler Registry components in a new application domain without modifying the core framework code.

A new event type is introduced to the system. A developer registers a new event handler through the Handler Registry without changing the Event Manager or Event Queue implementation, completing the modification within 2 hours.

The framework must accommodate a new business requirement for priority-based event handling. The modification is implemented by extending the event dispatching mechanism with minimal impact to existing handlers.
During normal operation, the Event Queue receives 100 events in rapid succession. The system processes all events in FIFO order without dropping or reordering any events.

A user submits an invalid event object (missing required fields) to the Event Manager. The system detects the invalid input, logs an error, and continues processing subsequent valid events without crashing or entering an inconsistent state.

A new developer joins the project and must understand the event flow from event submission to handler execution. The consistent event-driven paradigm allows the developer to trace the architecture within 4 hours of onboarding.

All framework components (Event Manager, Event Queue, Handler Registry) interact exclusively through the event dispatching mechanism, maintaining architectural coherence and preventing direct component coupling.

Under normal load conditions (50 events per second), the Event Manager dispatches events to registered handlers with an average latency of less than 10 milliseconds from queue insertion to handler invocation.

scenario_reference.text: A developer needs to reuse the Event Manager, Event Queue, and Handler Registry components in a new application domain without modifying the core framework code.

analysis_records.environment: Development environment with new application requirements
analysis_records.stimulus: Developer attempts to integrate framework into new application domain
analysis_records.response_measure: Framework components are reused without source code modification
architectural_decisions: Event Manager uses Handler Registry pattern for dynamic handler binding
Event Queue implements generic FIFO mechanism independent of event types
Handler Registry provides registration interface decoupled from core framework
analysis_records.reasoning: The registry pattern allows runtime binding of application-specific handlers to framework events. Generic queue and manager implementations avoid hard-coded dependencies on specific event types or handlers, enabling reuse across domains.

risks.description: Handler Registry may require domain-specific configuration that is not externalized
risks.rationale: If registry initialization logic is embedded in framework code, reuse requires modification

risks.description: Event Queue may have hard-coded assumptions about event structure
risks.rationale: Lack of abstraction for event representation limits reusability across different event schemas

non_risks.description: Event Manager's dispatching logic is domain-agnostic
non_risks.rationale: Manager operates on abstract event interface, not concrete implementations

scenario_reference.text: A new event type is introduced to the system. A developer registers a new event handler through the Handler Registry without changing the Event Manager or Event Queue implementation, completing the modification within 2 hours.

analysis_records.environment: Production system requiring new event type support
analysis_records.stimulus: New business requirement introduces new event type
analysis_records.response_measure: Handler added via registry in under 2 hours without core framework changes
architectural_decisions: Handler Registry provides registration API for runtime handler binding
Event Manager dispatches events through registry lookup, not hard-coded routing
Event Queue treats all events uniformly regardless of type
analysis_records.reasoning: Registry pattern decouples event-to-handler mapping from framework logic. Developer implements handler, registers it, and framework automatically routes events without modification to Event Manager or Queue.

risks.description: Handler Registry may not support dynamic registration without system restart
risks.rationale: If registry is initialized only at startup, adding handlers requires downtime

risks.description: Event Manager may cache handler mappings, preventing runtime updates
risks.rationale: Caching optimization could prevent new handlers from being recognized

non_risks.description: Event Queue does not require modification for new event types
non_risks.rationale: Queue operates on event interface, not concrete types

scenario_reference.text: The framework must accommodate a new business requirement for priority-based event handling. The modification is implemented by extending the event dispatching mechanism with minimal impact to existing handlers.

analysis_records.environment: System evolution requiring priority-based event processing
analysis_records.stimulus: Business requirement for priority event handling
analysis_records.response_measure: Priority mechanism added with minimal impact to existing handlers
architectural_decisions: Event Queue abstraction allows replacement with priority queue implementation
Event Manager dispatching logic is independent of queue ordering strategy
Handler Registry does not assume FIFO ordering
analysis_records.reasoning: Abstraction of queue implementation allows substitution of FIFO queue with priority queue. Event Manager and handlers interact through stable interfaces unaffected by queue ordering changes.

risks.description: Existing handlers may have implicit dependencies on FIFO ordering
risks.rationale: If handlers assume events arrive in submission order, priority queue breaks this assumption

risks.description: Event Queue interface may not support priority metadata
risks.rationale: If queue interface only accepts events without priority, extension requires interface changes

non_risks.description: Event Manager dispatching logic is order-agnostic
non_risks.rationale: Manager processes events as dequeued, regardless of ordering strategy

scenario_reference.text: During normal operation, the Event Queue receives 100 events in rapid succession. The system processes all events in FIFO order without dropping or reordering any events.

analysis_records.environment: Normal operation with burst of 100 events
analysis_records.response_measure: All events processed in FIFO order with no drops or reordering
architectural_decisions: Event Queue implements bounded or unbounded FIFO buffer
Event Manager processes events synchronously or with controlled concurrency
Queue provides thread-safe enqueue/dequeue operations
analysis_records.reasoning: FIFO queue guarantees ordering. Thread-safe operations prevent race conditions. Sufficient queue capacity or backpressure mechanism prevents drops during bursts.

risks.description: Bounded queue may overflow during burst, causing event loss
risks.rationale: If queue capacity is less than 100 and processing is slower than submission, events are dropped

risks.description: Concurrent event processing may violate FIFO ordering guarantees
risks.rationale: If multiple threads process events in parallel, completion order may differ from submission order

non_risks.description: FIFO queue data structure inherently maintains insertion order
non_risks.rationale: Standard queue implementation guarantees FIFO semantics

scenario_reference.text: A user submits an invalid event object (missing required fields) to the Event Manager. The system detects the invalid input, logs an error, and continues processing subsequent valid events without crashing or entering an inconsistent state.

analysis_records.environment: Normal operation with invalid user input
analysis_records.stimulus: Invalid event object submitted to Event Manager
analysis_records.response_measure: Error detected, logged, system continues processing without crash
architectural_decisions: Event Manager validates events before enqueuing
Error handling isolates invalid events from processing pipeline
Logging mechanism records validation failures
analysis_records.reasoning: Input validation at Event Manager boundary prevents invalid events from corrupting queue or handlers. Exception handling ensures graceful degradation rather than system failure.

risks.description: Invalid event may be enqueued before validation, corrupting queue state
risks.rationale: If validation occurs after enqueue, invalid events may cause handler failures

risks.description: Handler exceptions may propagate to Event Manager, halting event processing
risks.rationale: If handlers throw uncaught exceptions, Event Manager may stop processing queue

non_risks.description: Event Queue is isolated from validation logic
non_risks.rationale: Queue only stores validated events, preventing corruption

sensitivity_points.parameter: Exception handling scope in Event Manager

scenario_reference.text: A new developer joins the project and must understand the event flow from event submission to handler execution. The consistent event-driven paradigm allows the developer to trace the architecture within 4 hours of onboarding.

analysis_records.stimulus: New developer attempts to understand event flow
architectural_decisions: All components interact through uniform event dispatching mechanism
Event flow follows consistent pattern: submit -> enqueue -> dequeue -> dispatch -> handle
No alternative communication channels between components
analysis_records.reasoning: Consistent event-driven paradigm reduces cognitive load. Single interaction pattern eliminates need to learn multiple communication mechanisms. Clear component boundaries simplify tracing.

risks.description: Lack of documentation may obscure event flow despite architectural consistency
risks.rationale: Conceptual integrity does not guarantee discoverability without proper documentation

risks.description: Complex handler interactions may violate single-paradigm assumption
risks.rationale: If handlers communicate directly rather than through events, paradigm consistency breaks

non_risks.description: Event Manager provides single entry point for event flow tracing
non_risks.rationale: Centralized dispatching simplifies understanding of system behavior

sensitivity_points.parameter: Number of interaction patterns in system

tradeoff_points.parameter: Strict adherence to single event-driven paradigm
conflicting_attributes: Performance (negative: event overhead for all interactions)

scenario_reference.text: All framework components (Event Manager, Event Queue, Handler Registry) interact exclusively through the event dispatching mechanism, maintaining architectural coherence and preventing direct component coupling.

analysis_records.environment: Framework runtime with multiple component interactions
analysis_records.stimulus: Components need to communicate during event processing
analysis_records.response_measure: All interactions occur through event dispatching, no direct coupling
architectural_decisions: Components expose event-based interfaces only
No direct method calls between Event Manager, Queue, and Registry
Handler Registry accessed only through Event Manager
analysis_records.reasoning: Exclusive use of event dispatching enforces architectural boundaries. Prevents ad-hoc coupling that would violate event-driven paradigm and reduce conceptual integrity.

risks.description: Event Manager must directly access Handler Registry for handler lookup
risks.rationale: Registry lookup is synchronous operation that cannot be event-based without infinite recursion

risks.description: Performance requirements may force direct component coupling
risks.rationale: Event indirection overhead may be unacceptable for high-frequency operations

non_risks.description: Event Queue interaction is naturally event-based
non_risks.rationale: Queue enqueue/dequeue operations align with event dispatching paradigm

scenario_reference.text: Under normal load conditions (50 events per second), the Event Manager dispatches events to registered handlers with an average latency of less than 10 milliseconds from queue insertion to handler invocation.

analysis_records.environment: Normal load with 50 events per second
analysis_records.response_measure: Average latency < 10ms from queue insertion to handler invocation
architectural_decisions: Event Queue uses efficient FIFO data structure (e.g., linked list or ring buffer)
Handler Registry uses hash map for O(1) handler lookup
Event Manager minimizes processing overhead between dequeue and dispatch
analysis_records.reasoning: 10ms latency budget requires efficient queue operations (enqueue/dequeue), fast handler lookup, and minimal Event Manager processing. At 50 events/sec, average inter-arrival time is 20ms, providing sufficient processing window.

risks.description: Handler execution time may exceed latency budget
risks.rationale: If handlers take >10ms, total latency exceeds target even with zero framework overhead

risks.description: Handler Registry lookup may degrade with large number of event types
risks.rationale: If registry uses inefficient data structure, lookup time grows with registry size

risks.description: Event Queue contention under concurrent access may increase latency
risks.rationale: Lock contention in multi-threaded scenario adds unpredictable delays

non_risks.description: 50 events/sec load is manageable for modern systems
non_risks.rationale: Low event rate provides ample processing time per event

sensitivity_points.parameter: Handler Registry size (number of event types)

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (negative: increased complexity and error potential)
